design_philosophy.core_loop: Arena-based beat 'em up: move in 2D space, close gaps with enemies, execute punch/kick/grab combos to stun then finish isolated foes
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing multiple enemies converging on player; required to isolate targets through positioning and knockdowns to survive encounters
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Street brawler proving dominance through fighting prowess; restoring honor/rescuing loved one by defeating gang hierarchies
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Pioneering belt-scroll combat system combining horizontal AND vertical arena movement (distinct from left-right-only predecessors); combo-based damage model requiring sustained pressure rather than instant kills; semi-autobiographical designer vision rooted in real street fighting culture

difficulty.lives_system: Limited lives with DIP-configurable count; no continues available
difficulty.checkpointing: No mid-stage checkpoints; stage-to-stage progression only
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: None explicit; difficulty determined by arcade DIP settings (background parallax changes on NES between difficulty levels: daytime/sunset/nighttime) but player cannot adjust mid-play

progression.unlock_mechanism: Hidden level select codes available at title screen allowing non-linear stage access (Mission 2/3/4 skippable)
replayability_hooks: Hidden stage select enables speedrun/challenge routing
Difficulty variation (3 levels on NES) changes visual presentation and enemy behavior
Two-player simultaneous multiplayer (arcade/ports) creates cooperative replay value
Regional variant (JP vs US) offers thematically distinct character/setting aesthetics

world_building_techniques: Japanese version: Character-driven intro (Kunio chases kidnapped friend Hiroshi via motorcycle); establishes high school gang conflict context
US version: Minimal narrative framing; manual provides bare-bones setup ('beat up punks'); relies on visual localization (The Warriors aesthetic) to suggest street gang turf wars
Environmental storytelling via setting progressions (subway → streets → bar → boss lairs) implying escalating gang hierarchy confrontation
Ending sequence (JP only): Post-final-boss hand-shake reconciliation with rescued Hiroshi; completely removed in US release

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters present escalating gang hierarchy: rivals and lieutenants with individualized movesets must be isolated and stunned before executing finishing sequences; health gating prevents early stage damage (bosses push player away if health too high except Sabu)
notable_bosses: Riki/Jack (Stage 1): Kunio's future series rival; original blue school uniform + tape (JP) vs long-haired vest-wearing brawler with headband (US)
Shinji/Joel (Stage 2): Sleazy blue-uniformed yakuza member (JP) transformed to mohawked punker with mouth covering (US)
Misuzu/Kim (Stage 3): Sukeban schoolgirl gang leader (JP) redrawn as red cheongsam-wearing martial artist (US)
Sabu (Stage 4 final): Yakuza crime boss; unique mechanic—can be sat-on/collar-grabbed regardless of health (only boss exception)

faction_indicators: High school delinquent gangs (Japanese context): Kunio's crew vs rival schools and yakuza
Street gang territories (Western context): Four distinct gang factions controlling urban zones (inspired by The Warriors film aesthetic)
Sukeban/schoolgirl gangs represented as stage-specific faction (Stage 3)
Yakuza hierarchy culminating in Sabu as organized crime boss
danger_signals: Progression through escalating gang violence: lower-level minions → mid-tier officers → final yakuza authority
Environmental decay suggests gang-controlled spaces: shabby subway (Stage 1 US), street areas, bar establishments
Boss capture/kidnapping mechanics (girlfriend/Hiroshi abduction) as faction dominance markers
Crowd convergence during fights indicates gang territory incursion
environmental_storytelling: Shinjuku Station setting (JP) establishes real-world Tokyo gang context; transformed to anonymous subway in US to obscure origin
Bar signage (beer advertisements) marks adult gang hang-outs; localization retained these despite Nintendo censorship concerns
Daytime→sunset→nighttime background progression correlates with difficulty escalation, suggesting escalating danger/gang activity intensity
Final boss lair staging implies yakuza crime organization command center rather than street turf

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Renegade (NES) article (regional differences, level select codes, character redesigns, environmental changes)
Wikipedia - Renegade (video game) article (gameplay mechanics, combat system innovations, design philosophy, localization context)
